fact: October 13 is exactly 286 days into the year, which means there are only 79 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, squirrels are frantically burying nuts right now - they can bury up to 10,000 acorns in a single season and forget where half of them are!
The sun is setting almost 2 minutes earlier each day around October 13 in most places, making bedtime feel like it comes super fast.
Maple trees are dumping millions of helicopter seeds (called samaras) that spin like tiny tornadoes as they fall to the ground.
October 13 falls smack in the middle of Libra season, when the zodiac scales are supposed to be perfectly balanced - just like how day and night were almost equal a few weeks ago!
Woolly bear caterpillars are crawling everywhere right now, looking for cozy spots to hibernate - and some people think their brown stripes can predict how harsh winter will be!
The morning dew is getting heavier and stickier around this time because the air can't hold as much water when it gets colder at night.
In the Southern Hemisphere, October 13 means spring is in full swing and jacaranda trees are exploding with purple flowers that carpet the ground like nature's confetti.
Geese flying south right now can travel over 1,500 miles without stopping, and they honk constantly to keep their flock together like a really loud GPS system.
The moon around October 13 is getting close to being a full Hunter's Moon, which appears bigger and more orange because of all the dust and particles in the autumn air.
Spiders seem to be everywhere in houses right now because male spiders are desperately searching for mates before the first frost kills them.
October 13 is roughly 3 weeks after the autumn equinox, which means we're losing daylight fast - about 2.5 minutes per day in northern places!
